coredata.dc:title: Sensitively detection of ascorbic acid in orange samples with ionic liquid functionalized carbon dots and lanthanide complexes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ying
Xia, Ji-Ji
Lu, Jia-Zhen
Wang, Zi-Long
Wang, Mi-Tang
coredata.dc:description: In the food industry, ascorbic acid (AA) is widely used as an antioxidant. However, the concentration of AA is influenced by various factors, including temperature, time, and pH value during food preparation and storage, leading to its instability in content. This makes the accurate detection of AA content particularly important. Precise determination of AA content in food and pharmaceuticals is crucial for ensuring the adequate intake of essential vitamins and maintaining good health. Herein, we have developed a successful preparation method of an imidazole-based ionic liquid functionalized CDs hybrids APM-CDs. A novel photo functional hybrid probe Eu3+@APM-CDs was obtained via an ion-exchange reaction between APM-CDs and lanthanide complex Eu(DPA)3. The obtained hybrid probe was extensively characterized in detail. What's more, the results show that the fluorescent intensity of Eu3+@APM-CDs has a good linear response to AA concentrations in the range of 25–500 μM (R2 = 0.99), with a detection limit of 80 nM. The fluorescence probe demonstrates good selectivity and sensitivity toward AA and has the potential for significant application in AA detection by simplifying procedures and reducing interference from impurities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide probe
Ionic liquid
C-dots
Fluorescence sensor
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of APM functionalized CDs hybrid (APM-CDs)
Fluorescence sensing experiment of the detection of AA
Fluorescence and chemical sensing properties of Eu3+@APM-CDs
Mechanism of the interaction between AA and lanthanide

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of [APMIm][Br], APM-CDs, and Eu3+@APM-CDs
Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) Eu3+@APM-CDs and APM-CDs combined with AA, and (b) the stability of as prepared Eu3+@APM-CDs.
The fluorescence stability of the probe under different pH conditions and its trend in the coordinate system.

The quenching effect of ascorbic acid (AA) concentration ranging from 25 to 500 μM on the fluorescence intensity of the probe at 610 nm under 284 nm excitation was observed, alongside a linear relationship within this concentration range.
Changes in the fluorescence intensity at 610 nm of Eu3+@APM-CDs in the presence of 1 mM AA and its analogs under 284 nm excitation light.
Comparison of Eu3+@APM-CDs detection of AA with other methods.
Detection of AA content in real samples using the probe.

head.ce:title: Sensitively detection of ascorbic acid in orange samples with ionic liquid functionalized carbon dots and lanthanide complexes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemistry, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemistry, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Multiphase Flow and Heat Transfer in Power Engineering
School of Energy and Power Engineering
University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Multiphase Flow and Heat Transfer in Power Engineering, School of Energy and Power Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the food industry, ascorbic acid (AA) is widely used as an antioxidant. However, the concentration of AA is influenced by various factors, including temperature, time, and pH value during food preparation and storage, leading to its instability in content. This makes the accurate detection of AA content particularly important. Precise determination of AA content in food and pharmaceuticals is crucial for ensuring the adequate intake of essential vitamins and maintaining good health. Herein, we have developed a successful preparation method of an imidazole-based ionic liquid functionalized CDs hybrids APM-CDs. A novel photo functional hybrid probe Eu3+@APM-CDs was obtained via an ion-exchange reaction between APM-CDs and lanthanide complex Eu(DPA)3. The obtained hybrid probe was extensively characterized in detail. What's more, the results show that the fluorescent intensity of Eu3+@APM-CDs has a good linear response to AA concentrations in the range of 25–500 μM (R2 = 0.99), with a detection limit of 80 nM. The fluorescence probe demonstrates good selectivity and sensitivity toward AA and has the potential for significant application in AA detection by simplifying procedures and reducing interference from impurities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), or vitamin C, is crucial for sustaining normal physiological functions and overall health, acting as a key reducing agent and coenzyme in various metabolic activities. Its essential roles include protecting cells from oxidative damage, supporting immune function, aiding in collagen synthesis, and facilitating iron absorption. Accurate monitoring of AA levels is vital to prevent both deficiency, which can lead to conditions like scurvy and fatigue, and excess, which might cause issues such as diarrhea and kidney stones [1–3]. For example, AA provides protection against diseases caused by free radicals, such as Parkinson's disease and various cancers, as well as cellular damage [4]. A deficiency of AA in the human body can lead to skin bruising, gum bleeding, muscle weakness, joint swelling, and edema, while excessive intake of AA can cause kidney stones, diarrhea, and stomach cramps [5]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop detection methods for AA, which will be beneficial for the early prevention of these diseases.

Consequently, the current methods employed for detecting AA encompass High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [6], 2,6-dichloroindophenol titration, spectrophotometry [7], electrochemical methods [8], and more. Although these techniques are sensitive and highly specific, they also present certain limitations. They can be complex, time-consuming, and often require specialized and expensive equipment. In comparison, fluorescence assays are highly regarded due to their advantages of high sensitivity, wide linear range, and excellent selectivity. Yu et al. [9] designed and prepared a dual-emission lanthanide metal-organic framework (Eu0.07Gd0.03-MOF) that offers high sensitivity and colorimetric detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid at low detection limits. Zeng et al. [10] fabricated polymer nanoparticles ATP-Ce-Tris, which provide a valuable platform for AA analysis in clinical diagnostics and drug screening, demonstrating excellent sensitivity in the detection of AA in human serum samples.

Lanthanide ions exhibit remarkable spectral features, including unique f-f emission transitions, large Stokes shifts, high emission quantum yields, long lifetimes, narrow emission peaks, and more [11–13]. However, direct excitation of pure lanthanide ions is challenging due to their f-f transitions, which are spin-forbidden and typically have very low molar absorption coefficients in the ultraviolet–visible region. This hinders the absorption of excitation energy, resulting in weak luminescence and low luminescence efficiency for pure lanthanide ions. Sensitizing chromophores or the antenna effect has been demonstrated as an effective approach to address this issue [14–18]. 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) can form coordination bonds with lanthanide ions through its carboxyl and nitrogen atoms, and it can also sensitize the fluorescence of lanthanide ions through energy absorption by its benzene ring [19–21]. These properties indicate that DPA is an ideal fluorescence sensitizer for lanthanide ions.

Carbon dots (CDs) [22], have garnered significant attention due to their high fluorescence intensity [23], excellent photostability [24], low toxicity, high water solubility, and notable biocompatibility [25]. With continuous improvements in preparation techniques and surface functionalization, the original properties of CDs have been significantly optimized. Many researchers [26–28] have developed CDs doped with ionic liquids and lanthanide ions as fluorescent probes, which can effectively detect ascorbic acid (AA). All these carbon dot materials provide an attractive strategy for the detection of AA. Furthermore, Ionic liquids are a type of molten salt characterized by their low melting point, volatility, vapor pressure, wide liquid phase range, non-flammability, tunable polarity, high solubility, and stability [29]. Combining rare-earth complexes with ionic liquids not only effectively enhances the stability of rare-earth complexes but also often significantly improves their luminescence efficiency due to the presence of ionic liquids. However, in materials prepared using the above method, the content of lanthanide elements in ionic liquid luminescent materials is still relatively low. Overcoming this limitation can be achieved by “processing" rare-earth complexes into the anions or cations of ionic liquids through specific methods. Furthermore, ionic liquids can be combined with various substrate materials, such as carbon dots, clays [30], MOFs [31], COFs [32], and more. For instance, Wang et al. [33] prepared CDs terminated with [APMIm][Br], which exhibited high thermal stability and anion responsivity. Zhang et al. [34] employed 1-vinyl-3-butyl imidazolium cation [VBIm][BF4] as a functional monomer to enhance the selectivity of fluorescence probes for LC (fluorochloridone) in complex matrices. Wang and colleagues [35] utilized ionic liquid-functionalized carbon dots (IL@CDs) as functional monomers along with ofloxacin (OFL) to successfully synthesize a molecularly imprinted polymer fluorescence probe (CDs/Eu3+@MIP).

In this study, an imidazole-based ionic liquid hybrid fluorescence probe Eu3+@APM-CDs was designed for the direct detection of AA via an ion-exchange reaction between ionic liquid functionalized CDs (APM-CDs) and lanthanide complex Eu(DPA)3. Citric acid and [APMIm][Br]was used as a precursor to synthesize the ionic liquid functionalized CDs through the “bridge” of ILs. A new class of luminescent ‘soft’ materials has been developed, which could improve the luminescence performances as well as enhance the photochemical stability of the lanthanide complexes (Scheme 1 ). In the presence of carboxylic functional groups and ionic liquid cations, the Eu3+@APM-CDs sensor was formed through coordination and ion exchange. Moreover, it can be used for the detection of AA due to the luminescence property of the hybrid probe. Eu3+@APM-CDs has different fluorescence responses to AA and the analogs, including aspartic acid(Asp), glutamic acid,(Glu) glycine(Gly), threonine(Thr), phenylalanine(Phe), alanine(Ala), citric acid(CA), arginine(Arg), lysine(Lys), and fructose(Fru).The results show that the prepared samples can effectively monitor AA with high sensitivity and selectivity, the detection limit of Eu3+@APM-CDs to AA is 80 nM, indicating the hybrid material is feasible and reliable for AA sensing in food.

1-Methylimidazole, 3-Bromopropylamine hydrobromide were purchased from Adamas-Beta Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous ethanol, anhydrous ether and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, ascorbic acid, 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid, EuCl3·6H2O. Aspartate, glutamic acid, glycine, threonine, alanine, phenylalanine, arginine, lysine, fructose was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) in KBr pellets was obtained on a Nexus 912 AO446 spectrophotometer in the range of 4000-400 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on K-α+ supplied by Thermo Fisher Scientific. Among them, monochromatic Al Kα source was the X-ray source. Fluorescence spectra were measured with a RF-6000 spectrophotometer (wavelength resolution was 0.5 nm) using a xenon lamp as an excitation source.

The synthesis method of ionic liquid [APMIm][Br] is based on the previous reports [36] and the synthesis route is shown in Scheme 1. First, 1-methylimidazole (4.11 g, 50 mmol) and 3-bromopropylamine hydrobromide (10.95 g, 50 mmol) were heated in 50 mL of ethanol at 60 °C for 24 h under a nitrogen atmosphere to synthesize [APMIm][Br]. After cooling, the ethanol was evaporated, the viscous liquid was washed three times with anhydrous ether, and then vacuum dried at 80 °C for 24 h. The obtained white powder is the product ionic liquid [APMIm][Br]. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ 9.30 (t, J = 1.8 Hz, 1H), 7.86 (t, J = 1.8 Hz, 1H), 7.78 (t, J = 1.8 Hz, 1H), 4.34 (t, J = 6.8 Hz, 2H), 3.86 (s, 3H), 2.86–2.75 (m, 2H), 2.13 (p, J = 7.2 Hz, 2H). (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of APM functionalized CDs hybrid (APM-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of APM-CDs was optimized in accordance with a reported method [37]. First, citric acid (0.1 g) and [APMIm][Br] (0.367 g) were dissolved in a round bottom flask containing 10 mL of deionized water in a CA:IL ratio of 1:3. The mixture was sonicated to form a transparent solution. The solution was then poured into a 20 ml high-pressure reaction vessel lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the unreacted precursor molecules were removed by water dialysis using a dialysis bag (1000Da) for 3 days. Finally, the dialysate was freeze-dried to obtain brown powder-form ionic liquid modified CDs. The mixture was then stored for subsequent characterization and experiments.

10 mL solution of DPA in water (10 mM) and 1 mL of 100 mM Eu(NO3)3·6H₂O were mixed by magnetic stirring for 0.5 h. Subsequently, 5 mL of CDs (1 mg/mL) were added and 1 mL of 100 mM NaOH solution to adjust the pH to about 7, with stirring overnight at room temperature. Finally, the mixed solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 48 h (MW: 1000) to eliminate excess Eu, DPA, and Eu-DPA. The mixture was freeze-dried to obtain the product Eu3+@APM-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing experiment of the detection of AA
ce:section.ce:para: First, A transparent suspension (2 mg/ml) of Eu3+@APM-CDs was prepared by dissolving Eu3+@APM-CDs powder (20 mg) in 10 ml of deionized water and subjecting it to 5 min of ultrasonication. To prepare a series of parallel solutions, 400 μL of the Eu3+@APM-CDs solution and 100 μL of PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4) was mixed with varying concentrations of 4 mL ascorbic acid (AA) solution in 5 mL sample vials and stabilized at room temperature for 5 min. The changes in fluorescence intensity were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The concentration range of the added AA solution was 25–500 μM. The fluorescence measurements were carried out with an excitation wavelength of 284 nm and excitation and emission slit widths of 3 nm and 5 nm, respectively.

In the context of selective experiments, the preparation procedure of the Eu3+@APM-CDs solution remained consistent with the aforementioned methodology. However, additional variations were introduced by incorporating diverse solutions, including but not limited to aspartic acid, glutamic acid, glycine, threonine, alanine, citric acid, phenylalanine, arginine, lysine, and fructose.

To validate their surface structure and composition, detailed measurements of FTIR spectra and XPS spectra were conducted. Fig. 1 depicts the FT-IR spectra of the combinations of [APMIm][Br], APM-CDs, and Eu3+@APM-CDs. The peak at 3400 cm−1 in the [APMIm][Br] spectrum has been attributed to the N–H stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 3043 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of the methylene and methyl groups. Peaks observed at 1579, 1579, and 1609 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibrations of –COOH and –CONH, confirming the formation of amide linkages between the ionic liquid and C-dots. The absorption at 1386 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–N bond. The successful synthesis of the ionic liquid [APMIm][Br] was also verified by the 1H NMR spectra in Fig. S1.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has further revealed the all elements and the coordination interaction of the Eu3+@APM-CDs. Fig. 2 shows the test images of the X-ray spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum, with more details in Fig. S2. The survey spectrum revealed that the peaks are perceived at around 288.8 eV (C 1s), 401.1 eV (N 1s), 538.0 eV (O 1s). Three peaks in the high-resolution scan of C 1s at 284.2, 284.8, and 288 eV are ascribed to (C─C/C═C), (C–N/C–O), and (C═O), respectively [35]. The N 1s spectrum shows a peak at 398.8 eV corresponding to sp2-hybridized pyridine nitrogen and peak at 400.9 eV belonging to pyrrolic nitrogen of the APM-CDs. The O 1s spectrum shows two peaks at 531.1 and 532.4 eV could be assigned to O═C and C─O/C─OH, respectively. Fig. S2d displays the high-resolution Eu 3d spectrum; two peaks at 1134.1 and 1163.7 eV attributed to the Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2 were demonstrated the successful combination with Eu3+. In accordance with the findings in the FT-IR spectra, these results are consistent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and chemical sensing properties of Eu3+@APM-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of Eu3+@APM-CDs are given in Fig. S3. It's notable that the characteristic emission peak remains consistent across different excitation wavelengths, indicating that CDs maintain similar surface states and exhibit excitation-independent photoluminescent behavior. The luminescent excitation and emission spectra of Eu3+@APM-CDs were investigated at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S4a, under an excitation wavelength of 284 nm, a strong fluorescence emission peak was observed at 610 nm, exhibiting bright red luminescence under ultraviolet light, consistent with the position indicated in the CIE diagram of Fig. S4b. And four narrow characteristic emission spectra exhibit the 4f → 4f transitions of Eu3+ (5D0 → 7FJ; J = 1, 2, 3, 4), which located at 587, 610, 644, and 688 nm, respectively. The most intense emission peak at 610 nm is ascribed to 5D0→7F2 transition. Simultaneously, the feasibility of fluorescence sensing for AA molecules was explored. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, when AA molecules are introduced into the hybrid material, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+@APM-CDs at 610 nm significantly decreases. Fig. 3b shows that the probe can emit stable fluorescence when excited at 284 nm. These results verify the substantial potential of using the synthesized product for the detection of AA molecules.

To investigate the detection performance of the Eu3+@APM-CDs probe for AA, the photostability of the probe at different pH values was initially assessed. By adjusting the solution's pH, changes in the fluorescence intensity of the Eu3+@APM-CDs aqueous solution were analyzed, as depicted in Fig. 4 . The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ increased with rising pH and remained relatively stable within the pH range of 3–8. Subsequently, the detection capability of the Eu3+@APM-CDs probe for AA was studied. Fig. 5 illustrates the investigation of changes in the fluorescence properties of the probe Eu3+@APM-CDs by adding varying concentrations of AA solution. It can be observed that the luminescence of the carbon dots solution gradually diminishes as the concentration of AA solution increases. The range of study was chosen as 25–500 μM. Within this range, a well-defined linear relationship exists between the ratio of Fi/F0 and the concentration of AA. The fitting curve of the detection system can be calculated using the Stern-Volmer equation [38,39]: Fi/F0 = 1+ KSVC, where C is the concentration of ascorbic acid (AA), KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, Fi is the fluorescence intensity at Eu3+@APM-CDs after the addition of AA, and F0 is the fluorescence intensity of blank Eu3+@APM-CDs. The linear regression equation, as shown in the graph, is expressed as Fi/F0 = −0.0006664x+1.01948. (R2 = 0.99753). Based on 3σ/S, the detection limit (LOD) is 80 nM.

The Eu3+@APM-CDs synthesized in this study was compared with recent related research, as delineated in Table 1 . Notably, the Eu3+@APM-CDs fluorescent probe developed herein demonstrated superior sensitivity and a reduced detection threshold for ascorbic acid (AA) compared to many existing studies. These findings affirm that the Eu3+@APM-CDs probe exhibits outstanding fluorescence sensing capabilities for AA detection and shows significant potential as an effective sensor for AA concentration monitoring.

Fig. 6 provides a detailed illustration of our investigation into the selectivity of the Eu3+@APM-CDs sensor towards ascorbic acid (AA), using a range of AA and their analogs, including aspartic acid, glutamic acid, glycine, threonine, phenylalanine, alanine, citric acid, arginine, lysine, and fructose. The study indicates that while all analytes induced varying degrees of fluorescence enhancement, a notable fluorescence quenching effect was observed specifically with AA. We also assessed the anti-interference capabilities of Eu3+@APM-CDs probe with respect to these amino acids in the presence of AA, as detailed in Fig. S5. All solutions demonstrated significant fluorescence quenching, suggesting that Eu3+@APM-CDs could serve as a potential sensor with enhanced selectivity. Moreover, the anti-interference ability of Eu3+@APM-CDs for AA in the presence of other ion interferences is shown in Fig. S6. The results indicate that these ions do not pose any serious interference in the determination of AA. This significant effect is primarily attributed to the unique chemical structure of ascorbic acid, which includes active enol hydroxyl groups and other functional groups that interact specifically with active sites on the surface of the Eu3+@APM-CDs sensor. These interactions may lead to charge transfer or the formation of coordination bonds, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Additionally, the Eu element in the sensor, acting as a central metal ion, forms stronger coordination bonds with the DPA ligand. In the presence of both ascorbic acid and amino acids, due to its unique chemical structure, ascorbic acid may exhibit a higher affinity towards the Eu center, forming stronger coordination bonds compared to amino acids. This specific interaction between AA and the sensor is more pronounced. Therefore, the high selectivity of the Eu3+@APM-CDs sensor towards AA reflects the specificity of the interaction between its structural features and the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the interaction between AA and lanthanide
ce:section.ce:para: For the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Eu3+@APM-CDs by AA, the possible reason has been demonstrated in Fig. S7. Effective energy transfer exists within the hybrid probe Eu3+@APM-CDs, where Eu3+ can be efficiently excited, resulting in bright red emission. However, the presence of AA influences the efficiency of Ligand-to-Metal Energy Transfer (LMET), where Eu3+ ions coordinated with DPA experience competitive coordination due to AA, leading to luminescence quenching. To further comprehend this sensing process, UV–vis absorption spectra of AA and Eu3+@APM-CDs were measured, as depicted in Fig. S8. Eu3+@APM-CDs exhibits strong absorption bands within the range of 230–270 nm, overlapping with the absorption bands of AA within this range. Consequently, the competitive energy absorption between AA molecules and ligands reduces the efficiency of LEMT-induced fluorescence quenching.

l-ascorbic acid (VC) is a major active component in fruits, and its content is crucial for assessing the nutritional value of oranges. To evaluate the applicability and reliability of the proposed Eu3+@APM-CDs fluorescence system, real sample testing was conducted using fresh fruits to investigate the performance of the Eu3+@APM-CDs system in practical AA detection (See Table 2 ). The standard addition (recovery) method was employed to detect AA in fruit juices. The experimental procedure was as follows: Take 4 mL of fruit juice, such as orange or kiwifruit, diluted tenfold, and then perform no further treatment on the samples. After the addition of different concentrations of ascorbic acid (AA), 0.4 mL of previously prepared Eu3+@APM-CDs was added, and the fluorescence emission spectrum was measured using an excitation light of 284 nm. The recovery rates were between 93.8 % and 109.5 %, with an RSD % (n = 3) ranging from 0.01 % to 5.31 %. These results indicate that the fluorescence probe developed in this chapter is feasible for detecting in real samples.

In summary, a luminescent probe based on imidazolium-based ionic liquid-functionalized carbon dots was designed with FL quenching response to ascorbic acid through a hydrothermal method. This probe Eu3+@APM-CDs had a good specificity and quickly response towards AA. Moreover, via the testing of orange juice samples, this study demonstrated the high efficiency of Eu3+@APM-CDs fluorescent probe in detecting low concentrations of l-ascorbic acid, and verified its excellent sensitivity and adaptability in the analysis of low concentrations of AA. The fluorescence sensing method was developed for l-ascorbic acid detection in orange samples with good stability and precision, indicating that Eu3+@APM-CDs probe presented the great potential application value in the field of food science and nutrition analysis.

Ying Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Ji-Ji Xia: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Jia-Zhen Lu: Formal analysis, Data curation. Zi-Long Wang: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Mi-Tang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21101107, 51173107), State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers (Fudan University), State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse Foundation, (NO.PCRRF19017).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2024.112197.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a colorimetric sensing assay for ascorbic acid and sarcosine utilizing the dual-class enzyme activity of Fe3O4@SiO2@NiCo2S4

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xuchao W, Mengying C, Longshan Z. Development of a colorimetric sensing assay for ascorbic acid and sarcosine utilizing the dual-class enzyme activity of Fe3O4@SiO2@NiCo2S4 [J]. Chemical Engineering Journal, 2023, 468: 143612.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Can infrared spectroscopy detect Adulteration of Kakadu Plum (Terminalia ferdinandiana) dry powder with Synthetic ascorbic acid?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cozzolino D, Phan A D T, Aker S, et al. Can Infrared Spectroscopy Detect Adulteration of Kakadu Plum ( Terminalia ferdinandiana ) Dry Powder with Synthetic Ascorbic Acid? [J]. Food Analytical Methods, 2021, 14: 1936-1942.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical sensors for the detection of food adulterants in miniaturized settings
Nanosensing and Bioanalytical Technologies in Food Quality Control

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Periyasamy A, Selvam S, Chellakannu A, et al. Electrochemical Sensors for the Detection of Food Adulterants in Miniaturized Settings [J]. Nanosensing and Bioanalytical Technologies in Food Quality Control, 2022: 139-168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of trace ascorbic acid in urine by “inhibition” fluorescent sensor based on periodate and highly luminescent N, B co-doped carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li X, Chen G, Hu A, et al. Detection of trace ascorbic acid in urine by “inhibition” fluorescent sensor based on periodate and highly luminescent N, B co-doped carbon dots [J]. Dyes and Pigments, 2023, 218: 111454.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple and selective optical biosensor using Ultrasonicator synthesis of 5-((anthracen-9-ylmethylene) amino)-2,3-dihydrophthalazine-1,4-dione for direct detection of ascorbic acid in vegetables and fruits

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kathiravan S, Sundaram E, Paulraj B A, et al. Simple and selective optical biosensor using Ultrasonicator synthesis of 5-((anthracen-9-ylmethylene) amino)-2,3-dihydrophthalazine-1,4-dione for direct detection of ascorbic acid in vegetables and fruits [J]. Food Chemistry, 2020, 332: 127150.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: HPLC determination of erythorbate in cured meats

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lee K E N, Marder S. HPLC Determination of Erythorbate in Cured Meats [J]. Journal of Food Science, 1983, 48 (1): 306-306.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct spectrophotometric measurement of supra-physiological levels of ascorbate in plasma

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Witmer J R, Wetherell B J, Wagner B A, et al. Direct spectrophotometric measurement of supra-physiological levels of ascorbate in plasma [J]. Redox Biology, 2016, 8: 298-304.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical determination of ascorbic acid based on AgNPs/PVP‐modified glassy carbon electrode
